scenario.name: Iran-US Conflict: Strait of Hormuz Closure
scenario.description: Escalating Iran-US military conflict leads to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, choking ~20% of global oil supply and 40% of India's crude oil imports. India faces an acute energy crisis with cascading effects on inflation, forex reserves, manufacturing, and fiscal stability.
scenario.context: Following months of escalating tensions over Iran's nuclear programme, the United States launches targeted military strikes on Iranian nuclear and military installations. Iran retaliates by mining and blockading the Strait of Hormuz — the narrow chokepoint through which ~20 million barrels per day (roughly 20% of global oil supply) transit. Insurance companies suspend coverage for tankers transiting the Persian Gulf. Brent crude surges past $150/barrel within 72 hours. India, which imports ~88% of its crude oil (4.5+ million barrels/day), with ~40% sourced from Gulf nations via the Strait, faces an immediate supply disruption. The Indian rupee depreciates sharply, forex reserves come under pressure, and downstream industries from fertilisers to aviation face existential cost shocks. The crisis is expected to last 2-4 months before diplomatic resolution or alternative routing stabilises flows.

world_state.scenario_name: Iran-US Conflict: Strait of Hormuz Closure

ioc.name: Indian Oil Corporation (IOC)
ioc.description: India's largest commercial enterprise by revenue and the flagship national oil company. Operates the country's biggest refining network (80.7 MTPA), a vast fuel retail network (35,000+ outlets), and manages a significant share of India's strategic petroleum reserves. Central to India's energy security.
objectives: Ensure uninterrupted fuel supply across India despite supply disruption
Manage crude procurement costs and protect refining margins
Maintain strategic petroleum reserve levels for national energy security
Protect financial stability and shareholder value under extreme cost pressure
constraints: Government-regulated retail fuel pricing — cannot fully pass through costs
88% import dependency on crude oil — limited domestic alternatives
Pipeline and logistics infrastructure designed around Gulf crude grades
Refinery configuration optimized for specific crude baskets — switching grades has yield penalties
Strategic petroleum reserves cover only ~9.5 days of consumption

key_risks: Crude oil price volatility and supply disruption
Refinery utilisation drop if crude supply constrained

indian_govt_energy.name: Indian Government (Ministry of Petroleum & Finance)
indian_govt_energy.description: The Indian central government acting through the Ministry of Petroleum & Natural Gas and the Ministry of Finance. Responsible for energy security, fuel pricing policy, fiscal management, and macroeconomic stability during geopolitical crises.
objectives: Ensure energy security and prevent fuel shortages across India
Contain inflation and protect consumers from runaway fuel prices
Manage fiscal deficit — India's oil import bill is ~$150B/year at $80 crude
Maintain forex reserve adequacy and rupee stability
Diversify energy sourcing away from geopolitical chokepoints
constraints: Fiscal deficit target of 4.5% of GDP — limited room for fuel subsidies
Political economy — fuel price hikes trigger public backlash and electoral risk
Strategic petroleum reserves last only ~9.5 days at current consumption
Diplomatic balancing — India has ties with both US and Iran
Limited short-term alternatives to Gulf crude pipeline infrastructure
Monetary policy coordination needed with RBI on inflation and forex

policy_tools_available: Cut excise duty on fuel to cushion prices
Negotiate emergency crude supplies from Russia, US SPR, Africa
Impose fuel rationing or priority allocation
RBI forex intervention to stabilise rupee
Diplomatic engagement — ceasefire advocacy at UN/G20
Fast-track LNG import contracts and coal-to-power substitution

diplomatic_context.india_iran_relations: Strategic — Chabahar port, historical trade ties
diplomatic_context.india_us_relations: Strategic partner — defence, trade, Quad alliance
diplomatic_context.india_saudi_relations: Top crude supplier, strategic investment partner
diplomatic_context.india_russia_relations: Discounted crude supplier since 2022, strategic partner

indian_airlines.name: Indian Aviation Sector (IndiGo & Air India)
indian_airlines.description: India's aviation sector represented by its two largest carriers — IndiGo (market leader, ~60% domestic share) and Air India (Tata-owned, full-service international). Aviation turbine fuel (ATF) constitutes 35-45% of airline operating costs, making the sector acutely vulnerable to oil price shocks.
objectives: Maintain flight operations and route network despite ATF price surge
Protect operating margins and cash reserves under extreme fuel costs
Manage passenger demand impact from potential fare hikes
Honour fleet expansion commitments (IndiGo: 500+ aircraft order)
constraints: ATF is 35-45% of operating costs — $150 crude could mean 80%+ ATF price increase
Indian airlines cannot hedge fuel in domestic markets (regulatory restriction)
Fare increases face competitive pressure and demand elasticity
Fleet commitments — aircraft delivery payments in USD, exacerbated by rupee depreciation
Labour-intensive — cannot quickly cut capacity without workforce disruption
International routes require fuel stops that may be disrupted

sector_metrics.atf_sensitivity: Every $10/barrel increase adds ~INR 6,000/kl to ATF cost

key_risks: ATF price surge eroding margins or pushing to operating losses
Rupee depreciation increasing USD-denominated lease and capex costs
Demand destruction from fare hikes reducing load factors
International route disruption through Gulf airspace
Working capital squeeze from higher fuel procurement costs

indian_agri_fertilizer.name: Indian Agriculture & Fertilizer Sector
indian_agri_fertilizer.description: India's agriculture sector (employing ~42% of the workforce, contributing ~18% of GDP) and the fertilizer industry that sustains it. India imports ~30% of its urea and most of its potash and phosphate feedstock. Natural gas (a key feedstock for urea) and crude-linked naphtha are critical inputs. A Hormuz closure disrupts both energy costs and fertilizer supply chains, threatening food security.
objectives: Ensure uninterrupted fertilizer availability for the upcoming Kharif (monsoon) season
Contain input cost inflation for farmers — diesel, fertilizers, pesticides
Protect farm incomes and prevent rural distress
Maintain India's food grain production targets and buffer stock levels
constraints: Fertilizer subsidy bill already ~INR 1.88 lakh crore — fiscal room limited
Urea production depends on natural gas — 80% of gas is imported, much from Gulf
Phosphate and potash are 100% imported — supply chain via sea routes
Kharif sowing window (June-July) is non-negotiable — delays destroy crop yields
150+ million smallholder farmers — politically sensitive constituency
Diesel (farm mechanisation, transport) costs directly linked to crude price

energy_inputs.diesel_price_sensitivity: Every INR 10/litre increase adds INR 15,000/hectare to farming cost

key_risks: Fertilizer shortage during sowing season — crop failure risk
Diesel and transport cost spiral — farm gate price collapse vs retail inflation
Natural gas supply disruption halting urea plants
Rupee depreciation inflating all import-dependent input costs
Rural demand contraction cascading to FMCG, auto, banking sectors

rbi.name: Reserve Bank of India (RBI)
rbi.description: India's central bank responsible for monetary policy, inflation targeting (4% CPI ± 2%), financial stability, and forex reserve management. In an oil shock scenario, the RBI faces the classic impossible trinity — managing inflation, supporting growth, and defending the currency simultaneously.
objectives: Contain inflation within the 2-6% target band despite supply-side oil shock
Maintain orderly forex markets and prevent disruptive rupee depreciation
Preserve financial system stability — banks, NBFCs, and capital markets
Support economic growth without fuelling inflationary expectations
constraints: Inflation targeting mandate — CPI already at 5.5%, oil shock could push to 8-10%
Rate hikes to fight inflation would slow GDP growth (currently ~6.5%)
Forex reserves ($620B) are large but defending rupee burns reserves quickly
Capital outflows from FPIs during geopolitical risk reduce market liquidity
Transmission lag — rate changes take 6-9 months to affect real economy
Cannot control supply-side inflation with demand-side monetary tools

financial_stability.oil_company_borrowings_risk: Higher working capital needs from OMCs could stress banking system

policy_tools_available: Adjust repo rate (hike to fight inflation, cut to support growth)
Open market operations (OMO) — buy/sell government securities
Forex market intervention — sell USD from reserves to defend rupee
Special dollar swap windows for oil marketing companies
Forward guidance and communication to anchor expectations
Regulatory forbearance for stressed sectors (aviation, transport)

historical_precedent.2022_ukraine_shock: RBI hiked repo 250bps, spent $70B defending rupee, inflation peaked at 7.8%
historical_precedent.2008_oil_shock: Crude at $147 — RBI raised rates, rupee fell 25%, inflation hit 12%
historical_precedent.2014_oil_crash: Crude fell to $45 — RBI cut rates 150bps, rupee stabilised, growth recovered

all_signals.content: RBI issues cautionary note highlighting heightened inflation risks from oil-supply shock and affirms readiness to act decisively to preserve price stability.
Indian fertilizer sector activating non-Gulf supply chains and building record Kharif buffer to shield 150 million farmers from Hormuz shock.
RBI announces temporary capital-flow management measures; finance ministry rolls out limited fuel-cost support for buses, trucks and fertiliser units to protect farmers and commuters.
IOC monitoring Strait situation closely; no immediate commercial action announced.
IndiGo and Air India monitor fuel crisis closely, defer discretionary spend, and await policy direction while maintaining full schedule.
Indian fertilizer sector rushing to pre-position monsoon nutrients amid Gulf supply choke; urges government for interim freight-cum-subsidy support.
Indian Oil has entered into crude-oil and currency hedges covering the majority of its near-term import exposure to shield consumers and shareholders from extreme price volatility.
DGTR initiates anti-dumping investigation on Chinese flat-rolled steel imports to protect domestic industry from injury amid heightened external price volatility.
Govt announces temporary fuel-price stabilisation fund and accelerated switch to Rupee-denominated long-term contracts with Russia & Brazil to replace 0.6 mb/d of Gulf cargoes.
IndiGo & Air India monitoring fuel crisis closely; no immediate network changes announced.
Fertiliser industry seeks immediate government intervention to share the extraordinary energy-driven cost shock and protect farmer affordability for Kharif season.
RBI, acting via DGTR, imposes 20% provisional anti-dumping duty on Chinese flat-rolled steel for 120 days to counter injurious pricing during oil-shock window.

Government rolls out comprehensive energy-crisis package: duty-free LNG imports, enhanced LPG-fertiliser subsidies and forex stabilisation measures; RBI announces $10bn swap line and fresh NRI deposit facility to calm currency markets.
IndiGo & Air India monitoring fuel crisis; no new initiatives announced.
IOC executes emergency hedge to protect margins against crude price spike and currency volatility.

rbi.assessment: With CPI already at 5.5% and an oil shock that could push inflation to 8-10%, the immediate priority is anchoring inflation expectations. A public cautionary note signals vigilance without prematurely burning reserves or tightening policy before transmission lags play out. It buys time to assess persistence of the shock and coordinate with fiscal authorities on supply-side mitigation.

signals_to_ecosystem.content: RBI issues cautionary note highlighting heightened inflation risks from oil-supply shock and affirms readiness to act decisively to preserve price stability.

private_intent.content: Internal scenario runs show inflation breaching 8% within two months if pass-through is complete; preference is to conserve forex and await fiscal response before deploying rate or FX intervention tools.

ioc.assessment: With Brent at $1050/MT (≈$140/bbl) and rupee at 440/USD, every day of unhedged exposure widens our forex-linked crude payables. A -round 3-month swap on 70% of expected Gulf-shortfall volumes costs <3% cash, locks-in today’s Brent/INR, and caps worst-case cash bleed. Physical supply scramble will dominate later rounds; first priority is to ring-finance the P&L from super-spike volatility.

private_intent.content: Treasury executed Brent collars $135-$180/bbl on 10 mbbl and INR forwards at 445-460 for $2.2 bn notional; premium paid $42 m (2.1% cash).

rbi.assessment: With Brent at $1050/MT and INR under 440, the oil shock is already pushing import-cost inflation toward 8-10%. Under the RBI’s mandate to contain CPI within 2-6%, we must act now to curb any additional supply-side price pressure. Domestic steelmakers have filed a prima facie petition showing rising dumped import volumes from China at prices below normal value, threatening material injury. Opening a WTO-compliant anti-dumping investigation (max 12 months) allows provisional duties later if injury worsens, sending a hawkish signal that the central bank will not tolerate cost-push inflation spreading beyond energy. This preserves monetary credibility without burning reserves or hiking rates prematurely, buying time for fiscal authorities to secure alternative oil routes.

signals_to_ecosystem.content: DGTR initiates anti-dumping investigation on Chinese flat-rolled steel imports to protect domestic industry from injury amid heightened external price volatility.

private_intent.content: Use investigation timeline to stagger any provisional duty decision until after 2-month Hormuz window, aligning trade defence with inflation outlook and avoiding double supply shock.

Fiscal space exhausted, but letting farm-gate prices triple would crush Kharif acreage and food security. We must pass 30-35% of cost spike to keep plants running, while simultaneously pressing GoI for an emergency nutrient subsidy window or customs-duty waiver to cap retail MRPs.

signals_to_ecosystem.content: Fertiliser industry seeks immediate government intervention to share the extraordinary energy-driven cost shock and protect farmer affordability for Kharif season.

private_intent.content: Internal models show if we absorb >25% of this spike our cash reserves exhaust in 6 weeks; political backlash if retail prices double is existential.

rbi.assessment: With Brent at $1050/MT and INR at 440, the oil shock is feeding straight into landed cost of all imports. Domestic steelmakers face a 30-40% cost spike on ore, energy and freight, while artificially cheap Chinese offers (likely state-subsidised) flood in to grab market share. A 20% provisional duty for 4 months cushions the domestic industry during the Hormuz crisis, prevents dumping-induced closures and job losses, and is WTO-compliant under the ‘critical circumstances’ clause. It also signals that RBI will not let external price shocks destabilise strategic domestic capacity ahead of infra-led growth revival.

signals_to_ecosystem.content: RBI, acting via DGTR, imposes 20% provisional anti-dumping duty on Chinese flat-rolled steel for 120 days to counter injurious pricing during oil-shock window.

private_intent.content: Internal assessment shows 3 mt excess Chinese inventory looking for outlet; duty buys 90 days for domestic mills to reset contracts and pass through higher energy costs without dumping margin compression.

indian_govt_energy.assessment: Brent at $1050/MT (≈$150/bbl) pushes annual oil bill to ~$280B, risking 6% CAD and 8%+ inflation. Cannot let demand collapse or rupee free-fall. Launching targeted fiscal-monetary stimulus: zero customs duty on LNG/spot crude vessels, ₹600bn additional fertiliser & LPG subsidy, RBI to inject $10bn via forex swaps and raise FCNR(B) window to 3Y to draw NRI deposits. Simultaneously tightening capital-outflow ring-fence: cut FII debt redemption limit from 15% to 5%/month, mandate 100% margin on short-rupee OTC trades, and channel export-earnings repatriation through PSU banks within 30 days to stabilise INR/USD above 80.
